questionText: Compare these two playground prize games. Game R uses a fair bag with 2 rocket tokens, 3 ball tokens, and 5 sticker tokens. In 20 picks with replacement, rocket came out 7 times. Game S uses a fair bag with 3 rocket tokens, 2 ball tokens, and 5 sticker tokens. In 25 picks with replacement, rocket came out 10 times. Which game’s experimental probability for rocket is closer to its own theoretical probability?
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can compare theoretical and experimental probability
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Probability & Fractions: Theoretical Probability, aligned to Students can compare theoretical and experimental probability.
options: [{"text":"Game R, because 7 is closer to 2 than 10 is to 3.","correct":false},{"text":"Game S, because 10/25 is closer to 3/10 than 7/20 is to 2/10.","correct":true},{"text":"Game R, because 7/20 is greater than 2/10, so it must be closer.","correct":false},{"text":"Both games are equally close, because both experimental probabilities are greater than the theoretical probabilities.","correct":false}]
explanation: For Game R, the theoretical probability of rocket is 2/10 = 1/5 = 0.2. Its experimental probability is 7/20 = 0.35. The difference is 0.35 - 0.2 = 0.15. For Game S, the theoretical probability is 3/10 = 0.3. Its experimental probability is 10/25 = 2/5 = 0.4. The difference is 0.4 - 0.3 = 0.1. Since 0.1 is smaller than 0.15, Game S is closer to its theoretical probability. Option A compares counts instead of probabilities, option C wrongly assumes greater means closer, and option D ignores that the distances from theory are different.

questionText:
A bag has 20 tokens: 9 bone, 7 ball, and 4 paw. One token is picked and put back 40 times. The paw result satisfied all of these clues:
- it was fewer than the theoretical prediction,
- it was an even number less than 8,
- and it was exactly half of the theoretical number of paw picks expected in 40 picks.

What was the paw count?
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can compare theoretical and experimental probability
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Probability & Fractions: Theoretical Probability, aligned to Students can compare theoretical and experimental probability.
options: [{"text":"4","correct":true},{"text":"6","correct":false},{"text":"8","correct":false},{"text":"10","correct":false}]
explanation: The theoretical probability of paw is 4/20 = 1/5. In 40 picks, the theoretical number of paw picks is 40 × 1/5 = 8. The clue says the actual paw count was exactly half of the theoretical number, so it must be 4. This also fits the other clues because 4 is fewer than 8 and is an even number less than 8. The other options do not equal half of 8.

questionText: A fair spinner has 10 equal parts: 4 apple, 3 banana, and 3 strawberry. It was spun 40 times. The strawberry count was even, fewer than theory predicts, at least 1/5 of 40, and less than 1/4 of 40. Which number could be the strawberry count?
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can compare theoretical and experimental probability
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Probability & Fractions: Theoretical Probability, aligned to Students can compare theoretical and experimental probability.
options: [{"text":"6","correct":false},{"text":"8","correct":true},{"text":"10","correct":false},{"text":"12","correct":false}]
explanation: The theoretical probability of strawberry is 3/10, so the predicted number in 40 spins is 3/10 × 40 = 12. The count must be fewer than 12. Also, 1/5 of 40 = 8, so "at least 1/5 of 40" means 8 or more. And 1/4 of 40 = 10, so the count must be less than 10. So the count must be an even number that is at least 8, less than 10, and less than 12. Check the options: 6 is too small, 8 fits all the conditions, 10 is not less than 10, and 12 is not fewer than 12. So 8 is the only possible strawberry count.
